title: Mūlamadhyamakakārikā — The Fundamental Wisdom of the Middle Way (Nāgārjuna, tr. Jay L. Garfield)
subtitle: Nāgārjuna's foundational 27-chapter treatise on Madhyamaka philosophy — emptiness, dependent origination, and the middle way — with Garfield's translation and philosophical commentary

summary.one_line: Nāgārjuna's Mūlamadhyamakakārikā (c. 2nd-3rd century CE) is the foundational text of the Madhyamaka ('Middle Way') school of Mahāyāna Buddhism — a systematic analysis of emptiness (śūnyatā) as the ...
summary.scope: Covers all 27 chapters: (1) Conditions, (2) Motion, (3) Senses, (4) Aggregates, (5) Elements, (6) Desire and Attachment, (7) Origination, (8) Agent and Action, (9) The Perceiver, (10) Fire and Fuel, (11) Saṃsāra, (12) Suffering, (13) Compounded Phenomena, (14) Contact, (15) Essence, (16) Bondage, (17) Karma, (18) Self, (19) Time, (20) Causes, (21) Becoming, (22) Tathāgata, (23) Errors, (24) Four Noble Truths, (25) Nirvāṇa, (26) The Twelve Links, (27) Views. Jay Garfield's translation and commentary (1995) provides a philosophical reading informed by both Tibetan commentarial tradition and Western analytic philosophy.

sources.label: Jay L. Garfield (tr. and commentary) — The Fundamental Wisdom of the Middle Way: Nāgārjuna's Mūlamadhyamakakārikā (Oxford University Press, 1995). PDF, 394 pages, scanned.

body.text: The central thesis of the MMK is that emptiness (śūnyatā) is not nothingness but the absence of inherent existence (svabhāva). All phenomena exist — but they exist dependently, as products of causes and conditions. The famous verse (24:18): 'Whatever arises dependently is empty, that is dependently designated, and is the middle way.' Emptiness is not a separate reality to be realized beyond the world — it IS the nature of the world as dependently arisen. This directly contradicts the Śaiva claim that consciousness (caitanya) is a positive, self-existent reality. For Nāgārjuna, even consciousness is empty of inherent existence.

Chapter 24 presents the two truths: conventional truth (saṃvṛti-satya) and ultimate truth (paramārtha-satya). The ultimate truth is that all phenomena are empty of inherent existence. The conventional truth is that phenomena exist conventionally as dependently arisen. These are not two different realities but two perspectives on the same reality. The ultimate truth is not a higher reality to be attained — it is the correct understanding of the conventional. This is the key difference from Abhinavagupta's two levels (pure and impure path), where the pure path IS a higher reality (Śiva tattva) to be recognized. For Nāgārjuna, there is no higher reality — only the correct view of this one.

The MMK systematically refutes svabhāva (inherent existence, essence, self-nature) across 27 chapters. Each chapter examines a concept that is commonly assumed to have independent existence — conditions, motion, the senses, the aggregates, fire and fuel, the self, time, karma, the Tathāgata, nirvāṇa — and demonstrates that none can be found to exist inherently. The method is prasaṅga (reductio ad absurdum): assuming the opponent's position leads to contradictions. This is not a positive assertion of a new theory but a therapeutic deconstruction of all fixed views (dṛṣṭi). The purpose is not to establish a philosophical system but to cure the cognitive disease of reification.

Chapter 25 on nirvāṇa is one of the most radical in the MMK. Nirvāṇa is not a state to be attained or a place to be reached. It is the cessation of reification — the end of grasping at inherent existence. 'There is no distinction between saṃsāra and nirvāṇa; the limit of nirvāṇa is the limit of saṃsāra' (25:19-20). Liberation is not the achievement of a new condition but the correct understanding of the present one. This parallels the Tantraloka's recognitional model (pratyabhijñā) but without any positive substrate. For Abhinavagupta, nirvāṇa IS Śiva — a positive reality. For Nāgārjuna, nirvāṇa IS the cessation of the misconception of inherent existence.

Crucially, Nāgārjuna argues that emptiness itself is empty (24:11-14). If emptiness were a positive nature or a metaphysical principle, it would itself be a svabhāva — which would contradict the entire project. This is the 'emptiness of emptiness' (śūnyatā-śūnyatā). One cannot reify emptiness as a thing, a ground, a consciousness, or a reality. This is the sharpest possible contrast with Abhinavagupta's Śiva — which IS a positive reality (the 36th tattva, the 37th, the 38th). For Nāgārjuna, positing any positive ultimate (whether Śiva, Brahman, or even a 'luminous mind') is still a form of grasping. The ultimate is not a thing — it is the absence of misconception about things.

The Mūlamadhyamakakārikā is the most important text for understanding the philosophical difference between the Śaiva nondualism of Abhinavagupta and the Buddhist nondualism of the Madhyamaka. Both reject duality. Both reject the idea of a permanent, substantial self. But they differ on whether there IS a positive reality beyond the play of appearances. Nāgārjuna says no — even the idea of 'reality' is a reification. Abhinavagupta says yes — that reality is consciousness (caitanya), and it can be recognized. This debate remains the central philosophical unconcluded question between the two traditions. Garfield's commentary (drawing from the Tibetan Gelug tradition) takes the Nāgārjuna side, arguing that the Madhyamaka position is not nihilism but the middle way beyond eternalism and nihilism.

gaps: Full extraction of all 27 chapters with Garfield's commentary. PDF is scanned (no text layer) — requires OCR for full extraction.

issues.title: Scanned PDF requires OCR
issues.description: The MMK PDF (394 pages, 11 MB) is a scanned document with no text layer. Full extraction of the 27 chapters with Garfield's commentary requires OCR.

issues.title: Full comparative mapping to Tantraloka needed
issues.description: The MMK's 27 chapters each target a specific concept (conditions, motion, self, time, etc.) that has a parallel in the 36-tattva system. A chapter-by-chapter comparison would be valuable.

versions.summary: Initial RO created for Nāgārjuna's MMK with Garfield's commentary. 6 passages covering emptiness as dependent origination, the two truths, refutation of svabhāva, nirvāṇa and saṃsāra, emptiness of emptiness, and comparative significance. Full extraction requires OCR of the scanned PDF.

timeline.event: RO created from scanned PDF (11 MB, 394 pages). Text layer not accessible — OCR required for full extraction.
